questions.explanation: AIDS (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome), a syndrome, is caused by the retrovirus HIV (human immunodeficiency virus). The virus destroys a subgroup of lymphocytes, the helper T cells (or CD4 lymphocytes), resulting in suppression of the body’s immune response. HIV is transmitted in blood, semen, and vaginal fluid; the major routes of infection are unprotected vaginal and anal intercourse, intravenous drug abuse, and the administration of contaminated blood and blood products. A combination of antiviral drugs can delay the development of full-blown AIDS for many years but cannot fully cure the disease.

questions.text: HIV that causes AIDS first starts destroying:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because the AIDS retrovirus, called human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), mounts a direct attack on CD4+ T-helper cells because it recognises the CD4 coreceptors associated with these cells. HIV’s attack on CD4 + T cells cripples the immune system in at least three ways. First, HIV-infected cells die only after releasing replicated viruses that infect other CD4+ T cells, ultimately destroying the entire population of CD4+ T cells. Second, HIV causes infected CD4 + T cells, to secrete a soluble suppressing factor that blocks other T cells from responding to the HIV antigen. Finally, HIV may block transcription of MHC genes, hindering the recognition and destruction of infected CD4 + T cells, and thus protecting those cells from any remaining vestiges of the immune system. The combined effect of these responses to HIV infection is to wipe out the human immune defence.

questions.text: Which one of the following is the stage of cellular respiration for which oxygen is not essential:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A process in living organisms involving the production of energy, typically with the intake of oxygen and the release of carbon dioxide from the oxidation of complex organic substances is called respiration. Glycolysis which is the first step in all types of respiration is anaerobic and it does not require free oxygen. However, if oxygen is present aerobic respiration will occur and the pathway will continue to the Krebs cycle and oxidative phosphorylation. And if oxygen is not available, then Anaerobic respiration will occur.

During pyruvic oxidation, each pyruvate molecule loses a carbon dioxide (CO2) and is oxidized, producing one molecule of NADH. The remaining two-carbon fragment combines with coenzyme A (CoA) to form acetyl CoA. This process is an integral part of aerobic respiration and takes place in the mitochondrial matrix. It precedes the entry of acetyl CoA into the citric acid cycle, where further oxidation and energy production occur.

The Krebs cycle, also known as the citric acid cycle or tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle, is a key component of cellular respiration in aerobic organisms. It represents the extra volume of air your lungs can take in when you need to breath deeply. However, it is not directly related to preventing alveolar collapse. The IRV is typically used during strenous activites or when you need to take deep breath,such as during excercise.

Tidal volume is the volume of air that moves into and out of lungs during normal, quiet breathing. It is amount of air exchanged with each breath during regular, day-to-day activites. Tidal volume does not play a significant role in preventing alveolar collapse.

The expiratory reserve volume is the maximum amount of air that can be exhaled after completing a regular, calm exhalation. It signifies the additional air volume that your lungs can expel when necessary, such as during forceful exhalation or when you need to exhale more than usual. However,it is important to note that the EVR primarly serves to increase exhalation volume and doesn't have a direct role in preventing alveolar collapse. Instead, The primary function of EVR is to enhance the body's ability to expel more air when needed,as in activites requiring forceful breathing or when you need to clear your lungs of additional air.

The residual volume is the amount of air that stays within the lungs even following a vigorous exhalation. Its vital role lies in safeguarding the alveoli from complete collapse. This resudual volume effectively sustains a partial inflation of the alveoli, thereby preserving their structural integrity. It is a crucial factor in ensuring an uninterrupted exchange of gases within the lungs, Particularly during exhalation,when intra-thoracic pressure rises. This sustained volume of air in the alveoli prevents them from closing completely,which in turn facilitates continous gas exchange,ensuring that the respiratory system remains functional even after forceful exhalation.

questions.explanation: Even after a forceful expiration, a certain amount of air always remains in the lungs. This is called residual volume. It prevents the alveoli from collapsing completely, ensuring continuous exchange of gases (O₂ and CO₂) between breaths.

questions.text: Gradual breakdown of the alveolar wall leads to which type of disease in a smoker?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Narrowing of arteries to the heart → Heart problems
Inflammation of bronchial tubes → Mucus & cough
Destruction of alveoli → Poor gas exchangeThe gradual breakdown of the alveolar walls (tiny air sacs in the lungs)Loss of elasticity in lung tissue
Allergy-related airway narrowing and inflammation

questions.explanation: In emphysema, the inner walls of the lungs' air sacs (alveoli) are damaged, causing them to eventually rupture. This creates one larger air space instead of many small ones and reduces the surface area available for gas exchange. Emphysema is a lung condition that causes shortness of breath.Asthma is a condition in which your airways narrow and swell and may produce extra mucus. This can make breathing difficult and trigger coughing, a whistling sound (wheezing) when you breathe out and shortness of breath.Bronchitis is an infection of the main airways of the lungs (bronchi), causing them to become irritated and inflamed. The main symptom is a cough, which may bring up yellow-grey mucus (phlegm)Coronary heart disease (CHD), or coronary artery disease, develops when the coronary arteries become too narrow. The coronary arteries are the blood vessels that supply oxygen and blood to the heart.CHD tends to develop when cholesterol builds up on the artery walls, creating plaques. These plaques cause the arteries to narrow, reducing blood flow to the heart. A clot can sometimes obstruct the blood flow, causing serious health problems.

questions.text: Osteoporosis is due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Increased levels of estrogen would actually support bone density, making this option incorrect for osteoporosis, which is characterized by decreased estrogen levels.
This is correct as decreased estrogen levels, particularly after menopause, lead to reduced bone density, causing osteoporosis.
While calcium is important for bone health, an excess of calcium does not cause osteoporosis. Instead, osteoporosis is associated with a deficiency in bone density rather than an excess of calcium.
This is characteristic of gout, a distinct condition involving joint inflammation, not bone density loss as seen in osteoporosis.

questions.explanation: Post-menopausal women suffer from bone mass reduction due to reduced estrogen, leading to osteoporosis.

questions.text: Osteoarthritis is a _ disease.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An autoimmune disease is a condition where the immune system mistakenly attacks the body's own tissues, causing inflammation and damage. Examples include rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, and type 1 diabetes.

A hormonal disease refers to a medical condition characterized by an imbalance or dysfunction in the endocrine system, which produces and regulates hormones. Examples include diabetes mellitus, thyroid disorders, and adrenal gland disorders.

Nutritional diseases result from deficiencies, excesses, or imbalances in the intake of nutrients. Examples include scurvy (vitamin C deficiency), rickets (vitamin D deficiency), and obesity (excess calorie intake).

Degenerative diseases involve the gradual deterioration or loss of function of organs or tissues over time. Examples include osteoarthritis, Alzheimer's disease, and age-related macular degeneration. These conditions often result from aging, genetic factors, or a combination of both.

questions.explanation: Osteoarthritis is the most common form of arthritis. Some people call it a degenerative joint disease or “wear and tear” arthritis. It occurs most frequently in the hands, hips, and knees. Symptoms of this disease include pain, swelling, joint creaking, tenderness, and stiffness.

questions.text: The joint that allows movement in two directions is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fibrous joints are immovable joints that connect bones with dense fibrous connective tissue.
Cartilaginous joints allow limited movement and are connected by cartilage.

A hinge joint is a type of synovial joint that allows movement primarily in two directions, resembling the action of a hinge on a door. This design allows for flexion and extension movements. Hinge joints are found in various parts of the body, such as the elbow and knee joints.
Ball and socket joints allow movement in multiple directions, providing a wide range of motion.

questions.explanation: A hinge joint is a type of synovial joint that allows movement primarily in two directions, resembling the action of a hinge on a door. This design allows for flexion and extension movements. Hinge joints are found in various parts of the body, such as the elbow and knee joints.

questions.text: The collagen fibers of bone are hardened by deposit of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The collagen fibers of bone are hardened by the deposition of calcium phosphate, which forms the mineral component of bone tissue known as hydroxyapatite.
This option is incorrect. Calcium carbonate is a mineral that is found in some types of rocks and the shells of marine organisms, but it is not a major component of bone tissue.
Calcium oxalate is a compound that can form crystals in the urinary tract, but it is not involved in the hardening of bone tissue
Calcium bicarbonate is a compound that is formed when carbon dioxide dissolves in water, but it is not involved in the hardening of bone tissue.

questions.explanation: Explanation:The collagen fibers of bone are hardened by the deposition of calcium phosphate, which forms the mineral component of bone tissue known as hydroxyapatite.This option is incorrect. Calcium carbonate is a mineral that is found in some types of rocks and the shells of marine organisms, but it is not a major component of bone tissue.Calcium oxalate is a compound that can form crystals in the urinary tract, but it is not involved in the hardening of bone tissue.Calcium bicarbonate is a compound that is formed when carbon dioxide dissolves in water, but it is not involved in the hardening of bone tissue.

questions.text: Darwin observed and collected thousands of specimens of diverse:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because Darwin's observations included both animal and plant life, not just fauna.
This option is incorrect, as Darwin's research was not limited to flora; it also involved studying fauna.

options.text: Fauna and flora of South America
options.explanation: This option is correct because Darwin collected a wide range of both animal and plant specimens during his expedition in South America.

options.text: Fauna and flora of North America
options.explanation: This option is incorrect, as Darwin's main area of study was in South America, not North America.

questions.explanation: During his journey on the HMS Beagle, Charles Darwin meticulously observed and gathered a diverse collection of specimens, including both fauna and flora, from South America. His comprehensive study of these specimens, which were distinct from those in Europe, played a crucial role in the development of his theories on natural selection and evolution. Option C is correct, as it accurately reflects the breadth of his research. Options A and B are incorrect, as they focus solely on one type of specimen (either fauna or flora), while Option D is incorrect due to the erroneous geographical reference to North America.

questions.text: Origin of Species by Natural Selection included all except
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because 'descent with modification' is a key concept discussed in Darwin's 'On the Origin of Species,' describing how species change over time through inherited traits.
This option is incorrect because 'struggle for existence' is an essential part of Darwin's theory, explaining how individuals compete for limited resources.

This option is incorrect because 'survival of the fittest' is associated with Darwinism, referring to the process where individuals better adapted to their environment are more likely to survive and reproduce.
This option is correct because the concept of 'mutation' was not included in Darwin's original work. The role of mutations in evolution became known only after the development of modern genetics.

questions.explanation: Darwin's theory of natural selection, as presented in 'On the Origin of Species,' focuses on concepts such as 'Descent with modification,' 'Struggle for existence,' and 'Survival of the fittest.' These ideas highlight the processes of adaptation and selection in the evolution of species. However, the concept of 'Mutation' was not part of Darwin's original framework. The understanding of mutations as a source of genetic variation was developed later, with advances in genetics providing insight into the role of DNA and genetic changes in evolution.

questions.text: Who used the term "survival of the fittest"?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Jean-Baptiste Lamarck proposed the theory of inheritance of acquired characteristics, which was later discredited. He did not use the term 'survival of the fittest,' as his ideas predated Darwin's work.

Charles Darwin developed the theory of evolution through natural selection, but he did not initially use the phrase 'survival of the fittest.' The concept aligns with his theory, but the phrase was popularised by someone else.

Herbert Spencer coined the term 'survival of the fittest' in his 1864 book Principles of Biology to describe Darwin's concept of natural selection. He applied it broadly to biological evolution and societal progress.

Aristotle, an ancient Greek philosopher, had significant contributions to early biological thought but lived long before the concept of evolution by natural selection and did not use the term 'survival of the fittest.

questions.explanation: Herbert Spencer is credited with coining the phrase 'survival of the fittest' in his work Principles of Biology, published in 1864. He used this phrase to explain the process of natural selection described by Darwin, emphasizing that organisms better adapted to their environments are more likely to survive and reproduce. While Darwin's theory of natural selection underpins the concept, he did not initially use this exact phrase. Lamarck's ideas, predating Darwin, focused on acquired characteristics, not survival of the fittest. Aristotle's contributions to biology predate evolutionary theory entirely.

questions.text: The concept of uniformitarianism was formulated by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Aristotle was a philosopher who contributed significantly to various fields, but his work did not include the concept of uniformitarianism. His focus was on natural philosophy and biology.
Charles Darwin developed the theory of evolution by natural selection, which was influenced by uniformitarianism, but he did not formulate the concept himself.
Jean-Baptiste Lamarck is known for his theory of inheritance of acquired characteristics, which predates Darwin's work, but he did not contribute to the concept of uniformitarianism.
James Hutton is the correct answer. As a pioneering geologist, he introduced uniformitarianism, the idea that Earth's geological features are shaped by ongoing processes over long periods.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is James Hutton, often hailed as the father of modern geology. He introduced the concept of uniformitarianism, which posits that Earth's geological processes operate invariably over time. This principle laid the groundwork for understanding Earth's history in terms of gradual change, influencing later scientific theories like Darwin's evolution. The other options are incorrect: Aristotle focused on philosophy and biology, Lamarck proposed early evolutionary ideas unrelated to uniformitarianism, and Darwin built upon such geological concepts but did not originate them.

questions.text: What is the definition of "fitness" in terms of evolution?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The organism's ability to compete effectively for resources within its species
options.explanation: While competing for resources is important, it does not fully encompass fitness, as it lacks the crucial aspect of reproductive success. An organism's genetic impact is limited if it does not pass on its genes, regardless of its competitive prowess.

options.text: The organism's success in attracting mates
options.explanation: Mate attraction is a component of fitness, but it is insufficient on its own. Without successful offspring, an organism's genetic traits will not persist into future generations, thus reducing its evolutionary fitness.

options.text: The organism's overall health and well-being
options.explanation: While health can positively influence fitness, it is not a direct measure. An organism may be healthy but still fail to reproduce successfully, thereby not contributing genetically to future generations.

options.text: The ability of an organism to pass its genes to the next generation
options.explanation: In evolutionary terms, fitness is best defined by the organism's capacity to survive, reproduce, and ensure its genes are carried forward. This directly influences the genetic makeup of future generations, making it the correct definition of fitness.

questions.explanation: In evolutionary biology, 'fitness' is defined by an organism's ability to survive and reproduce, thereby ensuring its genetic traits are present in subsequent generations. Option D is correct, as it highlights this definition by emphasizing gene transmission. Option A, while touching on resource competition, fails to address the importance of reproduction. Option B focuses on mate attraction, which is part of fitness, but without successful reproduction, it does not fulfill the evolutionary goal. Option C regards health, which can affect fitness but does not define it unless it leads to reproductive success.

questions.text: Darwin's theory mainly focuses on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection does not explain how life originated. It addresses the changes in species over time once life is already present.
Darwin's theory does not focus on the extinction of individual organs but rather on the evolution and extinction of species as a whole.
Darwin’s theory primarily explains the process of natural selection, which leads to the evolution of new species by accumulating advantageous traits over generations.
The formation of individual organisms is not the focus of Darwin's theory; this is studied under developmental biology, which examines how organisms grow and develop.

questions.explanation: Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection primarily focuses on how new species arise through processes that increase the prevalence of advantageous traits within a population over time. These changes can eventually lead to the emergence of new species. The other options, such as the origin of life or the extinction of organisms, are not central to Darwin's theory. The formation of organisms is a topic explored by developmental biology, not evolutionary theory.

questions.text: Glucose reabsorption in the nephron occurs in the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Loop of Henle primarily focuses on creating a concentration gradient to facilitate water reabsorption. It does not play a significant role in glucose reabsorption.
The distal convoluted tubule is involved in the fine-tuning of ion balance and acid-base homeostasis, but not in the primary reabsorption of glucose.
The proximal convoluted tubule is the main site for glucose reabsorption in the nephron. Here, glucose is actively transported back into the bloodstream, preventing its loss in urine.
The collecting duct is responsible for the final concentration of urine and water reabsorption, but it does not engage in glucose reabsorption.

questions.explanation: The proximal convoluted tubule is critical for reabsorbing glucose from the filtrate back into the bloodstream, preventing glucose loss and maintaining homeostasis. This is achieved through active transport mechanisms. Other parts of the nephron, such as the Loop of Henle, distal convoluted tubule, and collecting duct, have different primary functions like water reabsorption, ion balance, and urine concentration, and do not significantly contribute to glucose reabsorption.

questions.text: A drug is used that prevents the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II. What is a likely effect of this drug?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Angiotensin II promotes sodium reabsorption in the kidneys. Blocking its formation would likely lead to decreased sodium reabsorption, not increased.

Inhibiting the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II decreases aldosterone secretion. Aldosterone usually promotes sodium reabsorption and potassium excretion. Therefore, decreased aldosterone levels would result in increased potassium reabsorption.
Angiotensin II is a potent vasoconstrictor that raises blood pressure. Inhibiting its production should result in decreased blood pressure, not increased.
Angiotensin II primarily affects blood pressure and electrolyte balance rather than blood pH. Thus, inhibiting its production would not directly alter blood pH.

questions.explanation: This question assesses knowledge of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system. Angiotensin II plays a crucial role in increasing blood pressure and promoting sodium reabsorption while facilitating potassium excretion through aldosterone secretion. By preventing the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, the pathway that leads to aldosterone release is disrupted. This results in decreased sodium reabsorption and increased potassium reabsorption due to reduced aldosterone levels.Option A is incorrect because angiotensin II promotes sodium reabsorption, which would decrease with its inhibition. Option C is incorrect because angiotensin II increases blood pressure; its inhibition would decrease blood pressure. Option D is incorrect because angiotensin II does not directly influence blood pH.

questions.text: This type of nephron is best to conserve water in body
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Juxtamedullary nephrons are specialized for water conservation due to their long loops of Henle that penetrate deep into the medulla, creating a steep osmotic gradient. This allows them to produce highly concentrated urine and conserve water efficiently.

Cortical nephrons primarily reside in the kidney's cortex and have shorter loops of Henle. They are less effective in concentrating urine and conserving water compared to juxtamedullary nephrons due to their structural limitations.
While both types of nephrons play roles in kidney function, juxtamedullary nephrons are specifically more effective in water conservation due to their ability to produce more concentrated urine.
The term 'serotical nephrons' is not recognized in kidney anatomy or physiology, making this option irrelevant to the question.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is juxtamedullary nephrons. These nephrons are particularly adept at conserving water due to their long loops of Henle, which extend deeply into the kidney medulla. This structural feature facilitates the creation of a high osmotic gradient, allowing for the production of concentrated urine. In contrast, cortical nephrons have shorter loops and are less effective in this regard. The option suggesting 'Both A and B' is incorrect because juxtamedullary nephrons have a distinct advantage in water conservation. 'Serotical nephrons' is not a valid term in the study of nephron function.

questions.text: This nephron segment is not permeable to water even in the presence of ADH
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The collecting duct's permeability to water is influenced by ADH; in its presence, it becomes permeable to water, allowing for water reabsorption and urine concentration.

options.text: Descending limb of loop of Henle
options.explanation: The descending limb of the loop of Henle is naturally permeable to water, irrespective of ADH presence, facilitating water movement out of the tubular fluid.

options.text: Ascending limb of loop of Henle
options.explanation: The ascending limb of the loop of Henle is impermeable to water, meaning that even in the presence of ADH, it does not allow water to pass through. Instead, it actively transports ions out of the tubular fluid.
This option incorrectly suggests both limbs of the loop of Henle are impermeable to water. While the ascending limb is indeed impermeable, the descending limb is permeable to water.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the ascending limb of the loop of Henle, which is impermeable to water even in the presence of ADH. This segment focuses on the active transport of sodium and chloride ions, thereby contributing to the osmotic gradient in the medulla. On the other hand, the collecting duct's permeability to water is regulated by ADH, and the descending limb of the loop of Henle is naturally permeable to water, allowing passive reabsorption. Thus, the other options do not match the condition described in the question.

questions.text: Active uptake of sodium is promoted by action of enzyme known as
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: "Adrenatrone" is not an enzyme or hormone recognized in human physiology related to sodium regulation.
Aldosterone is a key hormone produced by the adrenal glands that enhances sodium reabsorption in the kidney tubules, playing a critical role in maintaining sodium balance and blood pressure.
ADH primarily regulates water balance, not sodium. It increases water reabsorption in the kidneys, thus concentrating urine.
Diuretics are drugs, not hormones, that increase urine output. They generally promote the excretion of sodium and water, the opposite of sodium uptake.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Aldosterone. This hormone specifically promotes the active uptake of sodium in the kidneys, aiding in the body's fluid and electrolyte balance. Aldosterone acts on the distal tubules and collecting ducts to increase sodium reabsorption and potassium excretion, which helps regulate blood pressure and blood volume.Other options are incorrect because:Adrenatrone: Not a recognized substance associated with sodium regulation.Antidiuretic hormone (ADH): Primarily affects water reabsorption, not sodium uptake.Diuretic: Refers to substances that increase urine output, generally promoting sodium and water excretion, not uptake.

questions.text: A person who is on a long hunger strike and is surviving only on water, will have:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Less amino acids in his urine
options.explanation: This is incorrect because starvation leads to the breakdown of body proteins, increasing amino acid breakdown products in the urine.
This is incorrect because during prolonged fasting, blood glucose levels tend to decrease as glycogen stores are depleted to provide for the decreasing energy levels.
This is correct because starvation decreases urea production as the body uses proteins sparingly, reducing urea excretion.
This is incorrect because sodium levels are typically conserved during fasting to maintain electrolyte balance.

questions.explanation: During starvation, the body must rely on body proteins for the amino acids. On high protein diets the carbon skeletons of the amino acids are oxidized for energy or stored as fat and glycogen, but the amino nitrogen must be excreted. To facilitate this process, enzymes of the urea cycle are controlled at the gene level. When dietary proteins increase significantly, enzyme concentrations rise. On return to a balanced diet, enzyme levels decline. Under conditions of starvation, enzyme levels rise as proteins are degraded and amino acid carbon skeletons are used to provide energy, thus increasing the quantity of nitrogen that must be excreted in the form of urea.

questions.text: Excretion of dilute urine requires
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Relative permeability of the distal tubule to water
options.explanation: The distal tubule's permeability to water can influence urine concentration, but it is not the primary factor in producing dilute urine. This process mainly affects the concentration rather than dilution.

options.text: Transport of sodium and chloride ions out of the descending loop of Henle
options.explanation: The descending loop of Henle does not transport sodium and chloride ions; it is primarily permeable to water. The ascending loop, not the descending loop, is involved in ion transport, which helps create an osmotic gradient for urine concentration, not dilution.

Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) increases water reabsorption in the kidneys, leading to concentrated urine. Its absence, however, results in decreased water reabsorption and the production of dilute urine.

options.text: Impermeability of the collecting tubule to water
options.explanation: When the collecting tubule is impermeable to water, water is not reabsorbed, resulting in the excretion of dilute urine. This is the key mechanism for producing urine with a lower solute concentration.

questions.explanation: The excretion of dilute urine is primarily due to the impermeability of the collecting tubule to water. When the tubule does not allow water to pass, it results in the excretion of urine with a lower solute concentration. Other factors, like the permeability of the distal tubule and the presence of ADH, influence the concentration of urine, but the key factor for dilute urine is the impermeability of the collecting tubule. The transport of sodium and chloride ions in the loop of Henle is crucial for creating an osmotic gradient but does not directly produce dilute urine.

questions.text: Which enzyme is administered to the patients of Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Disease (SCID)?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: 'SCID' is due to Adenosine Deaminase (ADA) deficiency so, it is administered to overcome the deficiency. Hence, option A is correct.
Pancreatic enzymes (B) partake in digestion.
Beta-galactosidase breaks down lactose into glucose and galactose in certain organisms.
Beta-lactamase is an enzyme produced by bacteria, that confers their resistance against beta-lactam drugs, such as penicillin, as it breaks down the beta-lactam ring present in these drugs.

questions.explanation: 'SCID' is due to Adenosine Deaminase (ADA) deficiency so, it is administered to overcome the deficiency. Hence, option A is correct. Pancreatic enzymes (B) partake in digestion. Beta-galactosidase (C) breaks down lactose into glucose and galactose in certain organisms. Beta-lactamase (D) is an enzyme produced by bacteria, that confers their resistance against beta-lactam drugs, such as penicillin, as it breaks down the beta-lactam ring present in these drugs.

questions.text: The process of replacing or supplementing the defective allele with a functional, normal allele is known as _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This term is not commonly used in biotechnology. Alleles can be transferred, but the process is not typically called an 'allele transplant.'
Physiotherapy involves physical methods to promote health and wellness and is unrelated to genetic modification.
Gene therapy involves replacing or supplementing defective genes with functional ones to treat genetic disorders. This process can be applied to somatic or germline cells.
Mutation refers to changes in the DNA sequence, which can be harmful, beneficial, or neutral, but does not involve replacing defective alleles with normal ones.
Cloning creates genetically identical copies of a cell or organism and is not about replacing defective alleles.

questions.explanation: Gene therapy is an experimental technique that uses an allele to treat or prevent disease,by replacing the defective allele. In the future, this technique may allow doctors to treat a disorder by inserting a gene into a patient's cells instead of using drugs or surgery.

questions.text: Genes of interest can be selected from a genomic library by using:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cloning vectors are used to insert foreign DNA fragments, they are not specifically designed for selecting genes of interest from a genomic library.
This option is correct because A DNA probe is a single-stranded fragment of DNA that contains a nucleotide sequence specific for the gene or chromosomal region of interest in a genomic library.
Gene targets are not commonly used tools for selecting genes of interest from a genomic library.
Restriction enzymes are used to cut DNA at specific sequences, they are not used for selecting genes of interest from a genomic library.

questions.explanation: A gene bank or genomic library is a complete collection of cloned DNA fragments that comprises the entire genome of an organism. Molecular probes are small DNA segments that are used to detect the presence of complementary sequences in nucleic acid samples in the genomic library. These are usually formed of 200­500 nucleotide sequences. These segments or probes are labeled either with radioactive or nonradioactive compounds. Probes with DNA sequences complementary to the gene to be isolated are used. They bind with the desired gene, making it visible and help in isolating it from the library.

questions.text: A 16 weeks pregnant woman, who already has a son with haemophilia, visits a genetic counselor to know about the genetic health of her second child.The diagnostic test that the genetic counsellor refers to the woman would be
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ultrasound is a non-invasive imaging technique that can provide information about the developing fetus, including its size, anatomy, and general health. However, it is not a diagnostic test for genetic disorders like hemophilia. Ultrasound may be used for monitoring the pregnancy and assessing the physical development of the fetus.

Genetic therapy involves interventions to treat or correct genetic disorders. However, it is not typically performed during pregnancy for the purpose of diagnosing genetic conditions in the fetus. Genetic therapy is more oriented toward treating or preventing genetic disorders after birth.

Tissue cultures involve growing cells in a laboratory setting. While they can be used for various purposes in genetics research, they are not a common diagnostic test during pregnancy to determine the genetic health of the fetus

Amniocentesis is an invasive prenatal diagnostic test that involves the sampling of amniotic fluid surrounding the fetus. This fluid contains fetal cells, and genetic analysis of these cells can provide information about the presence of genetic disorders, including hemophilia. Amniocentesis is often recommended in cases where there is an increased risk of genetic conditions, such as when there is a family history of a genetic disorder like hemophilia.

questions.explanation: In the given scenario, given the family history of hemophilia and the woman's desire to know about the genetic health of her second child, amniocentesis would be a suitable diagnostic test to analyze the fetal genetic material for the presence of hemophilia or other genetic conditions. It allows for a more definitive diagnosis of genetic disorders during pregnancy. It's worth noting that amniocentesis is an invasive procedure and carries some risks, so the decision to undergo the test should be made after careful consideration and counseling.

questions.text: In the context of gene therapy, what is the primary goal of ex vivo gene therapy?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: To directly modify genes within a patient's body
options.explanation: This describes in vivo gene therapy, not ex vivo, as it involves modifying genes directly inside the body.

options.text: To remove and modify cells outside the patient's body before reintroduction
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. Ex vivo gene therapy involves taking cells out of the body, modifying them in a controlled environment, and then reintroducing them to the patient.

options.text: To introduce foreign genes into a patient's genome
options.explanation: While gene introduction can be part of gene therapy, this option does not specify the ex vivo process of modifying cells outside the body.
Cloning cells for transplantation relates to therapeutic cloning, distinct from the process of ex vivo gene therapy.

questions.explanation: In ex vivo gene therapy, the primary goal is to remove cells from a patient, modify or correct genetic defects outside the body, and then reintroduce the altered cells back into the patient. This method allows for precise manipulation in a controlled environment, reducing the risk of unintended genetic changes. Option B accurately describes this process. Option A refers to in vivo gene therapy, where modifications occur directly inside the body. Option C is too broad and does not specify the ex vivo procedure. Option D is unrelated to gene therapy, focusing on cloning for transplantation purposes.

questions.text: In the context of gene therapy, what is the main challenge in the development of viral vectors for gene delivery?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Ensuring the virus can replicate in the patient's cells
options.explanation: This is incorrect. While replication of the virus might occur, it is usually undesirable as it can lead to uncontrolled viral activity and potential harm to the patient.

options.text: Preventing the virus from integrating into the patient's genome
options.explanation: This is a challenge but not the main one. Integration can cause mutations, but the primary concern is ensuring safe and efficient delivery of the therapeutic gene.

options.text: Achieving efficient and targeted gene delivery without adverse effects
options.explanation: Correct. The primary challenge in developing viral vectors for gene therapy is ensuring that the gene is delivered efficiently and specifically to the target cells without causing adverse effects.

options.text: Overcoming immune responses to the virus
options.explanation: This is a significant consideration, as immune responses can negate the effectiveness of gene delivery; however, it is not the central challenge faced in viral vector development.

questions.explanation: The main challenge in the development of viral vectors for gene therapy is achieving efficient and targeted gene delivery without adverse effects. Viral vectors must be able to deliver the therapeutic gene to the correct cells and express it at therapeutic levels without causing harm to the patient. Other challenges include preventing immune responses and avoiding unintended integration into the host genome, which can lead to mutations. However, these are secondary compared to the need for safe and efficient delivery.

questions.text: In the process of iron rusting, which of the following is the limiting reactant?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Iron is a finite quantity and acts as limiting reactant.
Atmosphere contains 21% of oxygen.
Air is readily available in the atmosphere.
The atmosphere also contains moisture which is responsible for rusting.

questions.explanation: Iron would be the limiting reactant because air and moisture are readily available while the iron itself is of finite quantity, and would therefore be depleted first. The limiting reagent in a chemical reaction is a reactant that is totally consumed when the chemical reaction is completed. The amount of product formed is limited by this reagent since the reaction cannot continue without it.

questions.text: In the process of iron rusting, which of the following is limiting reactant?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is correct, as the limiting reactant refers to the reactant that is completely consumed first and determines the maximum amount of product that can be formed. In the case of iron rusting, the primary reactants involved are iron (Fe), oxygen (O2), and water (H2O). The reaction can be represented as:4 Fe + 3 O2 + 6 H2O = 4 Fe(OH)3If the iron is a limiting reactant, the iron will not be able to fully react, and the formation of rust will be limited.
Oxygen is necessary, but usually present in excess in the environment It is not limiting unless under special conditions (e.g., sealed containers)

questions.text: The anions (A) form hexagonal closest packing (HCP) and the cations (C) occupy only 2/3 of the octahedral holes. The simplest formula of the ionic compound is:

To find the simplest formula of the ionic compound based on the crystal structure described, we follow these steps:Given:Anions (A⁻) form hexagonal closest packing (HCP).Cations (C⁺) occupy 2/3 of the octahedral voids.Key Concept:In HCP packing, the number of octahedral voids = number of atoms (in this case, anions A⁻).So, for 1 anion, there are 1 octahedral void.If 2/3 of octahedral voids are filled with cations, then:For 1 anion, there are (2/3) cations.Let’s Assume:Number of anions = 3Then:Number of octahedral voids = 3Number of cations = (2/3) × 3 = 2Therefore:Cations : Anions = 2 : 3→ Simplest formula = C₂A₃

questions.explanation: The simplest formula of the ionic compound is determined by the arrangement and filling of ions in the crystal lattice. Here, anions (A) form a hexagonal closest packing (HCP) structure, which provides an equal number of octahedral voids. Since cations (C) occupy 2/3 of these octahedral holes, for every 3 anions, there will be 2 cations. Therefore, the correct formula is C2A3, reflecting the 2:3 ratio of cations to anions.The other options are incorrect because they do not correctly represent the ratio derived from the given conditions. CA suggests a 1:1 ratio, C3A2 suggests a 3:2 ratio, and C4A3 suggests a 4:3 ratio, none of which match the conditions provided.

options.explanation: Chemical bonds are forces that hold atoms together within a molecule. These forces involve the sharing or transfer of electrons between atoms to form stable molecules. Intermolecular forces, on the other hand, refer to the forces of attraction between molecules, not within a single molecule.

Intramolecular forces are the forces that hold atoms together within a molecule. This is similar to option (a). Intermolecular forces, as mentioned earlier, are forces between different molecules, not within a single molecule.

The intermolecular forces are typically referred to as Van der Waals forces. These forces are the attractive or repulsive forces that exist between molecules or atoms near one another. However, they are still important in determining the properties of substances, such as their boiling and melting points, viscosity, and solubility.

options.explanation: Henry's law relates to the solubility of gases in liquids and the pressure above the liquid, not to energy changes in reactions.

Joule's principle deals with the conversion of electrical energy into heat, specifically in resistive materials, and is not related to chemical reaction pathways.

questions.explanation: Hess's Law is a fundamental principle in thermochemistry, asserting that the total enthalpy change during a chemical reaction is the same regardless of the number of steps in which the reaction is carried out, because enthalpy is a state function. This means that it depends only on the initial and final states of the system, not on the path taken.Henry's law, Dalton's law, and Joule's principle all deal with different principles: Henry's law is about gas solubility in liquids, Dalton's law is about partial pressures in gas mixtures, and Joule's principle is about the conversion of electrical energy to heat in conductors. None of these involve the calculation of enthalpy changes in chemical reactions.

questions.text: In the reaction A + B -> Products if B is taken in excess then it is an example of:

options.explanation: a) Second order reaction:A second-order reaction refers to a reaction whose rate depends on the concentrations of two reactants, raised to the power of two. However, in the given reaction A + B -> Products, the rate of the reaction is not explicitly dependent on the concentration of B. Therefore, this option is not applicable.

b) Zero order reaction:A zero-order reaction is a reaction in which the rate is independent of the concentration of the reactants. In such a reaction, the rate equation would not include any concentration terms. However, in the given reaction A + B -> Products, the rate of the reaction is expected to be influenced by the concentration of B, as B is taken in excess. Therefore, this option is not applicable.

A pseudo-unimolecular reaction or pseudo first order reaction s a reaction whose rate depends on the concentration of a single reactant, raised to the power of one. The rate equation typically includes a term like [A] or [B]. In the given reaction A + B -> Products, the rate of the reaction is expected to be dependent on the concentration of both A and B. However, if B is taken in excess, its concentration remains relatively constant, and the rate of the reaction becomes primarily dependent on the concentration of A. Therefore, the given reaction can be approximated as a pseudo first-order reaction or pseudo-unimolecular reaction with respect to A when B is taken in excess.

questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The C-I bond has the lowest bond energy among the options due to iodine's low electronegativity, resulting in a weaker bond.
While the C-H bond is strong, it is not the strongest among the options. The electronegativity difference here is less than that of C-F.
The C-Cl bond is stronger than C-I but weaker than C-F due to chlorine's moderate electronegativity.
C-F has the highest bond energy due to fluorine's high electronegativity, which results in a very strong bond.

questions.explanation: The C-F bond has the highest bond energy because fluorine is the most electronegative element, which leads to a very strong bond due to significant bond polarity. The high electronegativity of fluorine allows it to attract bonding electrons more effectively than other halogens, forming a stronger and more stable bond. In comparison, the C-I bond is the weakest due to iodine's low electronegativity. The C-H bond is strong, but the electronegativity difference is not as pronounced as in C-F, while the C-Cl bond is stronger than C-I but weaker than C-F.

questions.text: In which of the following reaction is the rate of reaction not affected by increasing concentration of nucleophile?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: SN2 (substitution nucleophilic bimolecular) reaction: The rate of an SN2 reaction is influenced by the concentration of the nucleophile. As the concentration of the nucleophile increases, the rate of the reaction also increases.

E2 (elimination bimolecular) reaction: Similar to the SN2 reaction, the rate of an E2 reaction is affected by the concentration of the nucleophile. As the concentration of the nucleophile increases, the rate of the reaction also increases.

SN1 (substitution nucleophilic unimolecular) reaction: The rate of an SN1 reaction is not affected by the concentration of the nucleophile. The rate-determining step in an SN1 reaction involves the formation of a carbocation intermediate, which is independent of the concentration of the nucleophile. The rate of the reaction is primarily determined by the stability of the carbocation intermediate.
This option suggests that none of the given reactions have a rate that is unaffected by increasing the concentration of the nucleophile. However, the correct answer is C) SN1, as explained above.

Reacting a Grignard reagent with acetaldehyde or any aldehyde other than formaldehyde results in a secondary alcohol, not a primary alcohol.
Grignard reagents react with ketones to form tertiary alcohols after acidic hydrolysis, not primary alcohols.
Methyl chloride does not undergo a reaction to form an alcohol in the presence of a Grignard reagent. It serves as a precursor in the formation of the Grignard reagent itself.

questions.explanation: The Grignard Reaction involves the addition of an organomagnesium halide (Grignard reagent) to carbonyl compounds. On the other hand, 2o alcohols are typically produced by the reduction of ketones, not aldehydes. 3o alcohols would require a more complex starting compound, usually not involving aldehydes. questions.explanation: The correct answer is Chromophore. Chromophores are the parts of a dye molecule responsible for color production. questions.text: When a wave goes from one medium to another medium, which one of the following characteristics of the wave remains constant?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The velocity of a wave changes when it goes from one medium to another. The velocity of a wave depends on the properties of the medium through which it travels. When a wave enters a different medium, its velocity may increase or decrease, depending on the properties of the new medium.

The frequency of a wave remains constant as it moves from one medium to another. Frequency is the number of complete oscillations (cycles) of the wave that occur per unit time. It is a fundamental property of the wave and remains unchanged regardless of the medium it travels through.

The wavelength of a wave does not remain constant when it goes from one medium to another. Wavelength is the distance between two successive points in a wave that are in phase (e.g., crest to crest or trough to trough). It is determined by the source of the wave and remains the same as it moves through different media.

The phase of a wave changes when it enters a different medium. Phase refers to the position of a point on a wave cycle relative to a reference point (e.g., crest to trough or any other point on the wave). The change in the medium can cause a phase shift in the wave.

questions.explanation: The frequency of a wave remains constant as it moves from one medium to another. Frequency is the number of complete oscillations (cycles) of the wave that occur per unit time. questions.text: The radiations all around us are called _ radiations.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Natural radiations: Natural radiations refer to the various forms of radiation that occur in the environment without human intervention. These radiations come from natural sources like the Sun (which emits electromagnetic radiation, including visible light and ultraviolet radiation), cosmic rays from outer space, and radioactive elements found in rocks and soil. Some examples of natural radiation include sunlight, gamma rays from space, and radon gas emissions from the ground.

Artificial radiations: Artificial radiations are those that are produced by human activities. These radiations are not naturally present in the environment but are created through various processes and technologies. Some common sources of artificial radiation include X-rays and gamma rays used in medical imaging and radiation therapy, radio waves from communication devices like cell phones, and radioactive isotopes used in research, industry, and medicine.

Both A or B: This option suggests that the radiations around us can be either natural or artificial, depending on their source. As explained earlier, natural radiations come from natural sources, while artificial radiations are human-made.

No radiation is around us: This option is incorrect. Radiation is present all around us in various forms, as mentioned in options a) and b). Natural sources, as well as artificial sources, contribute to the radiation that we encounter in our daily lives.

questions.explanation: Humans come into contact with radiation on a daily basis. These radiations are both natural, such as the rays from the sun, and artificial, such as those from our TV screens or kitchen stoves. This radiation that we experience on a daily basis is known as background radiation
